title: Delay with result of comparison causes invalid codegen
body:
Having a delay statement with the result of a comparison of two integers causes the generated C++ code to reference a non-existent function and thus leads to a compilation error.

To reproduce, compile the following code with ``verilator --timing --trace --exe --build -j `nproc` --binary invalid_codegen_delay.sv -o invalid_codegen_delay`` (using the git master version):
```systemverilog
module top();
    int intvar;
    int intvar2;

    initial begin
        #(intvar < intvar2);
    end
endmodule
```

This produces an error during C++ compilation:
```
Vinvalid_codegen_delay___024root__DepSet_h44aef0d8__0.cpp: In function ‘VlCoroutine Vinvalid_codegen_delay___024root___eval_initial__TOP__Vtiming__0(Vinvalid_codegen_delay___024root*)’:
Vinvalid_codegen_delay___024root__DepSet_h44aef0d8__0.cpp:23:40: error: ‘VL_LTS_QII’ was not declared in this scope; did you mean ‘VL_LTS_III’?
   23 |     co_await vlSelf->__VdlySched.delay(VL_LTS_QII(32, vlSelf->top__DOT__intvar, vlSelf->top__DOT__intvar2),
      |                                        ^~~~~~~~~~
      |                                        VL_LTS_III
```

Of course, this code is somewhat nonsensical (I meant to use a `wait` statement when I was writing the original code that caused this issue), so I'm not sure if this is supposed to compile in the first place (since I would expect the argument to the delay statement to be of time type and not integral type...). If it isn't, Verilator should still throw an error during verilation, though.
